---
title: "Web Scraping Basics"
subtitle: "Lesson 16"
---

::: {.learning-objectives}
#### Learning Objectives

After this lesson, you will be able to:

- Understand when web scraping is appropriate (vs. APIs)
- Use BeautifulSoup to parse HTML documents
- Navigate the DOM to extract specific elements
- Handle nested structures and tables
- Practice ethical scraping (robots.txt, rate limiting)
:::

## Introduction

Web scraping extracts data from web pages by parsing their HTML. While APIs are preferred, scraping is sometimes necessary when:

- No API exists
- The API is too limited
- You need historical snapshots of web content

::: {.callout-warning}
#### Ethical Scraping

Before scraping any website:

1. **Check for an API first** (it's almost always better)
2. **Review the site's `robots.txt` file** (e.g., example.com/robots.txt)
3. **Respect rate limits** to avoid overwhelming servers
4. **Check terms of service** for scraping policies
5. **Cache responses** to minimize requests
6. **Identify yourself** with a User-Agent header
:::

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import requests
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup
import time
from pathlib import Path
```

## BeautifulSoup Basics

BeautifulSoup parses HTML and provides methods to navigate and search the document.

```{python}
# pip install beautifulsoup4

# Sample HTML (like a simple job listing page)
sample_html = """
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head><title>Job Listings</title></head>
<body>
    <h1>UW-Madison Job Classifications</h1>
    <div class="job-list">
        <div class="job" id="job-1">
            <h2 class="title">Assistant Professor</h2>
            <span class="code">FA001</span>
            <span class="salary-range">$85,000 - $120,000</span>
            <p class="description">Teaching and research position.</p>
        </div>
        <div class="job" id="job-2">
            <h2 class="title">Associate Professor</h2>
            <span class="code">FA002</span>
            <span class="salary-range">$95,000 - $150,000</span>
            <p class="description">Senior faculty position.</p>
        </div>
        <div class="job" id="job-3">
            <h2 class="title">Academic Staff</h2>
            <span class="code">AS001</span>
            <span class="salary-range">$55,000 - $85,000</span>
            <p class="description">Research and support role.</p>
        </div>
    </div>
</body>
</html>
"""

# Parse the HTML
soup = BeautifulSoup(sample_html, "html.parser")
print(f"Page title: {soup.title.string}")
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## In R
```r
# rvest approach
library(rvest)
page <- read_html("https://example.com")
titles <- page %>%
  html_elements("h2") %>%
  html_text()
```
:::

### Finding Elements

```{python}
# Find by tag name
h1 = soup.find("h1")
print(f"H1 text: {h1.text}")

# Find by class
jobs = soup.find_all("div", class_="job")
print(f"Number of jobs: {len(jobs)}")

# Find by ID
job1 = soup.find(id="job-1")
print(f"Job 1 title: {job1.find('h2').text}")
```

### CSS Selectors

CSS selectors provide a powerful way to find elements:

```{python}
# Select by class
titles = soup.select(".title")
print("Job titles:")
for title in titles:
    print(f"  - {title.text}")

# Select nested elements
codes = soup.select(".job .code")
print("\nJob codes:")
for code in codes:
    print(f"  - {code.text}")

# Complex selectors
first_job_title = soup.select_one("#job-1 .title")
print(f"\nFirst job: {first_job_title.text}")
```

### Common Selector Patterns

| Selector | Meaning | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `tag` | Element by tag | `soup.select("div")` |
| `.class` | Element by class | `soup.select(".job")` |
| `#id` | Element by ID | `soup.select("#job-1")` |
| `parent child` | Descendant | `soup.select(".job .title")` |
| `parent > child` | Direct child | `soup.select(".job-list > .job")` |
| `[attr]` | Has attribute | `soup.select("[href]")` |
| `[attr=value]` | Attribute equals | `soup.select("[class='title']")` |

## Navigating the DOM

The DOM (Document Object Model) is a tree structure. Navigate it using parent, child, and sibling relationships.

```{python}
# Get a job element
job = soup.find("div", class_="job")

# Navigate to children
for child in job.children:
    if child.name:  # Skip text nodes
        print(f"Child: {child.name} - {child.text.strip()[:30]}")
```

```{python}
# Navigate to parent
title = soup.find("h2", class_="title")
parent = title.parent
print(f"Title's parent: {parent.name}, id={parent.get('id')}")

# Navigate to siblings
code = soup.find("span", class_="code")
next_elem = code.find_next_sibling()
print(f"After code: {next_elem.text}")
```

## Extracting Structured Data

Let's extract job data into a DataFrame:

```{python}
def extract_job_data(soup):
    """Extract all job listings into a list of dictionaries."""
    jobs = []

    for job_div in soup.find_all("div", class_="job"):
        job = {
            "id": job_div.get("id"),
            "title": job_div.find("h2", class_="title").text.strip(),
            "code": job_div.find("span", class_="code").text.strip(),
            "salary_range": job_div.find("span", class_="salary-range").text.strip(),
            "description": job_div.find("p", class_="description").text.strip()
        }
        jobs.append(job)

    return jobs

jobs = extract_job_data(soup)
df = pd.DataFrame(jobs)
print(df)
```

### Parsing Salary Ranges

```{python}
import re

def parse_salary_range(salary_str):
    """Extract min and max salary from a range string."""
    # Remove $ and commas, find all numbers
    numbers = re.findall(r"[\d,]+", salary_str)
    numbers = [int(n.replace(",", "")) for n in numbers]

    if len(numbers) >= 2:
        return numbers[0], numbers[1]
    elif len(numbers) == 1:
        return numbers[0], numbers[0]
    return None, None

# Apply to our data
df["salary_min"], df["salary_max"] = zip(*df["salary_range"].apply(parse_salary_range))
print(df[["title", "salary_min", "salary_max"]])
```

## Scraping Tables

HTML tables are common and easy to parse:

```{python}
table_html = """
<table class="salary-table">
    <thead>
        <tr>
            <th>Division</th>
            <th>Headcount</th>
            <th>Median Salary</th>
            <th>Total Budget</th>
        </tr>
    </thead>
    <tbody>
        <tr>
            <td>Letters & Science</td>
            <td>2,450</td>
            <td>$78,500</td>
            <td>$245,000,000</td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
            <td>Medicine</td>
            <td>3,200</td>
            <td>$95,000</td>
            <td>$420,000,000</td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
            <td>Engineering</td>
            <td>1,100</td>
            <td>$88,000</td>
            <td>$125,000,000</td>
        </tr>
    </tbody>
</table>
"""

soup = BeautifulSoup(table_html, "html.parser")
```

### Method 1: Manual Extraction

```{python}
def extract_table(soup, table_selector):
    """Extract an HTML table into a list of dictionaries."""
    table = soup.select_one(table_selector)

    # Get headers
    headers = [th.text.strip() for th in table.select("thead th")]

    # Get rows
    rows = []
    for tr in table.select("tbody tr"):
        cells = [td.text.strip() for td in tr.select("td")]
        rows.append(dict(zip(headers, cells)))

    return rows

data = extract_table(soup, ".salary-table")
df = pd.DataFrame(data)
print(df)
```

### Method 2: pandas read_html

```{python}
# pandas can read HTML tables directly!
tables = pd.read_html(table_html)
df = tables[0]  # First table found
print(df)
```

```{python}
# Clean numeric columns
def clean_currency(val):
    """Convert currency string to number."""
    if pd.isna(val):
        return val
    return int(str(val).replace("$", "").replace(",", ""))

df["Median Salary"] = df["Median Salary"].apply(clean_currency)
df["Total Budget"] = df["Total Budget"].apply(clean_currency)
df["Headcount"] = df["Headcount"].str.replace(",", "").astype(int)

print(df)
print(df.dtypes)
```

## Fetching Web Pages

Now let's fetch real web pages:

```{python}
#| eval: false
def fetch_page(url, headers=None, delay=1):
    """Fetch a web page with polite delay."""
    default_headers = {
        "User-Agent": "UW-Salary-Analysis/1.0 (Educational project; contact@example.com)"
    }
    headers = headers or default_headers

    time.sleep(delay)  # Be polite
    response = requests.get(url, headers=headers, timeout=30)
    response.raise_for_status()

    return BeautifulSoup(response.text, "html.parser")

# Example usage
# soup = fetch_page("https://example.com/jobs")
```

### Checking robots.txt

```{python}
#| eval: false
from urllib.parse import urlparse

def check_robots_txt(url):
    """Check if scraping is allowed by robots.txt."""
    parsed = urlparse(url)
    robots_url = f"{parsed.scheme}://{parsed.netloc}/robots.txt"

    try:
        response = requests.get(robots_url, timeout=10)
        print(f"robots.txt for {parsed.netloc}:")
        print(response.text[:500])  # First 500 chars
    except requests.RequestException as e:
        print(f"Could not fetch robots.txt: {e}")

# check_robots_txt("https://www.wisconsin.edu")
```

## Handling Complex HTML

Real-world HTML is often messy. Here are strategies for common issues:

### Missing Elements

```{python}
messy_html = """
<div class="job">
    <h2 class="title">Position A</h2>
    <span class="code">CODE1</span>
    <!-- No salary range! -->
</div>
<div class="job">
    <h2 class="title">Position B</h2>
    <!-- No code! -->
    <span class="salary-range">$50,000 - $60,000</span>
</div>
"""

soup = BeautifulSoup(messy_html, "html.parser")

def safe_extract(element, selector, default=""):
    """Safely extract text from an element."""
    found = element.select_one(selector)
    return found.text.strip() if found else default

jobs = []
for job_div in soup.find_all("div", class_="job"):
    jobs.append({
        "title": safe_extract(job_div, ".title"),
        "code": safe_extract(job_div, ".code", "UNKNOWN"),
        "salary": safe_extract(job_div, ".salary-range", "Not specified")
    })

print(pd.DataFrame(jobs))
```

### Nested Structures

```{python}
nested_html = """
<div class="department">
    <h2>College of Letters & Science</h2>
    <div class="sub-department">
        <h3>Chemistry</h3>
        <ul class="employees">
            <li>John Smith - $95,000</li>
            <li>Jane Doe - $102,000</li>
        </ul>
    </div>
    <div class="sub-department">
        <h3>Physics</h3>
        <ul class="employees">
            <li>Bob Wilson - $98,000</li>
        </ul>
    </div>
</div>
"""

soup = BeautifulSoup(nested_html, "html.parser")

employees = []
for dept in soup.find_all("div", class_="department"):
    college = dept.find("h2").text.strip()

    for sub in dept.find_all("div", class_="sub-department"):
        sub_name = sub.find("h3").text.strip()

        for emp in sub.find_all("li"):
            name, salary = emp.text.rsplit(" - ", 1)
            employees.append({
                "college": college,
                "department": sub_name,
                "name": name.strip(),
                "salary": salary.strip()
            })

print(pd.DataFrame(employees))
```

## Pagination

Many sites split data across multiple pages:

```{python}
#| eval: false
def scrape_paginated(base_url, max_pages=10, delay=1):
    """Scrape multiple pages of results."""
    all_data = []

    for page in range(1, max_pages + 1):
        url = f"{base_url}?page={page}"
        print(f"Fetching page {page}...")

        try:
            soup = fetch_page(url, delay=delay)

            # Extract data from this page
            page_data = extract_job_data(soup)

            if not page_data:
                print("No more data, stopping.")
                break

            all_data.extend(page_data)

        except requests.RequestException as e:
            print(f"Error on page {page}: {e}")
            break

    return all_data

# Example usage
# all_jobs = scrape_paginated("https://example.com/jobs", max_pages=5)
```

## Caching Scraped Data

Cache pages locally to avoid re-fetching:

```{python}
import hashlib

def fetch_with_cache(url, cache_dir="data/scrape_cache"):
    """Fetch page with local file caching."""
    cache_path = Path(cache_dir)
    cache_path.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    # Create unique filename from URL
    url_hash = hashlib.md5(url.encode()).hexdigest()
    cache_file = cache_path / f"{url_hash}.html"

    # Check cache
    if cache_file.exists():
        print(f"Using cached: {url[:50]}...")
        return BeautifulSoup(cache_file.read_text(), "html.parser")

    # Fetch and cache
    print(f"Fetching: {url[:50]}...")
    response = requests.get(url, timeout=30)
    response.raise_for_status()

    cache_file.write_text(response.text)
    return BeautifulSoup(response.text, "html.parser")
```

## Complete Scraping Pipeline

```{python}
class JobScraper:
    """Scraper for job listing pages."""

    def __init__(self, cache_dir="data/scrape_cache", delay=1):
        self.cache_dir = Path(cache_dir)
        self.cache_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        self.delay = delay

    def _get_cache_path(self, url):
        """Generate cache filename."""
        url_hash = hashlib.md5(url.encode()).hexdigest()
        return self.cache_dir / f"{url_hash}.html"

    def fetch(self, url, use_cache=True):
        """Fetch a page with optional caching."""
        cache_path = self._get_cache_path(url)

        if use_cache and cache_path.exists():
            return BeautifulSoup(cache_path.read_text(), "html.parser")

        time.sleep(self.delay)
        response = requests.get(
            url,
            headers={"User-Agent": "Educational-Scraper/1.0"},
            timeout=30
        )
        response.raise_for_status()

        if use_cache:
            cache_path.write_text(response.text)

        return BeautifulSoup(response.text, "html.parser")

    def extract_jobs(self, soup):
        """Extract job data from a page."""
        jobs = []
        for job_div in soup.find_all("div", class_="job"):
            jobs.append({
                "title": safe_extract(job_div, ".title"),
                "code": safe_extract(job_div, ".code"),
                "salary_range": safe_extract(job_div, ".salary-range")
            })
        return jobs

    def scrape_all(self, base_url, max_pages=10):
        """Scrape all pages."""
        all_jobs = []

        for page in range(1, max_pages + 1):
            url = f"{base_url}?page={page}"

            try:
                soup = self.fetch(url)
                jobs = self.extract_jobs(soup)

                if not jobs:
                    break

                all_jobs.extend(jobs)
                print(f"Page {page}: {len(jobs)} jobs")

            except Exception as e:
                print(f"Error on page {page}: {e}")
                break

        return pd.DataFrame(all_jobs)
```

::: {.pycharm-tip}
When developing scrapers, use PyCharm's debugger to inspect the `soup` object. Set a breakpoint after parsing and use the Variables pane to explore the DOM structure interactively.
:::

## Exercise {#exercise}

::: {.exercise-box}
#### Exercise 16: Scraping a Salary Table

Given this sample HTML representing salary data:

```{python}
exercise_html = """
<html>
<body>
    <h1>2024 Salary Report</h1>
    <table id="salary-data">
        <thead>
            <tr>
                <th>Name</th>
                <th>Department</th>
                <th>Title</th>
                <th>Salary</th>
            </tr>
        </thead>
        <tbody>
            <tr class="row faculty">
                <td>Alice Johnson</td>
                <td>Chemistry</td>
                <td>Professor</td>
                <td>$125,000</td>
            </tr>
            <tr class="row faculty">
                <td>Bob Smith</td>
                <td>Physics</td>
                <td>Associate Professor</td>
                <td>$98,500</td>
            </tr>
            <tr class="row staff">
                <td>Carol Williams</td>
                <td>Chemistry</td>
                <td>Research Scientist</td>
                <td>$72,000</td>
            </tr>
            <tr class="row staff">
                <td>David Brown</td>
                <td>Biology</td>
                <td>Lab Manager</td>
                <td>$65,000</td>
            </tr>
        </tbody>
    </table>
</body>
</html>
"""
```

Your tasks:

1. Parse the HTML and extract all salary data into a DataFrame
2. Convert the Salary column to numeric
3. Calculate the average salary by department
4. Find all faculty members (hint: check the row class)
5. Calculate the salary gap between faculty and staff

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Solution

```{python}
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup
import pandas as pd
import re

# 1. Parse HTML and extract salary data
soup = BeautifulSoup(exercise_html, "html.parser")

def extract_salary_table(soup):
    """Extract salary table into DataFrame."""
    table = soup.find("table", id="salary-data")
    rows = []

    for tr in table.select("tbody tr"):
        cells = tr.find_all("td")
        row_class = tr.get("class", [])

        rows.append({
            "name": cells[0].text.strip(),
            "department": cells[1].text.strip(),
            "title": cells[2].text.strip(),
            "salary_str": cells[3].text.strip(),
            "employee_type": "faculty" if "faculty" in row_class else "staff"
        })

    return pd.DataFrame(rows)

df = extract_salary_table(soup)
print("1. Extracted data:")
print(df)

# 2. Convert Salary to numeric
def parse_salary(s):
    """Convert '$125,000' to 125000."""
    return int(re.sub(r"[^\d]", "", s))

df["salary"] = df["salary_str"].apply(parse_salary)
print("\n2. With numeric salary:")
print(df[["name", "salary"]])

# 3. Average salary by department
avg_by_dept = df.groupby("department")["salary"].mean()
print("\n3. Average salary by department:")
print(avg_by_dept)

# 4. Find all faculty members
faculty = df[df["employee_type"] == "faculty"]
print("\n4. Faculty members:")
print(faculty[["name", "title", "salary"]])

# 5. Salary gap between faculty and staff
avg_by_type = df.groupby("employee_type")["salary"].mean()
gap = avg_by_type["faculty"] - avg_by_type["staff"]
print("\n5. Salary gap analysis:")
print(f"   Faculty average: ${avg_by_type['faculty']:,.0f}")
print(f"   Staff average:   ${avg_by_type['staff']:,.0f}")
print(f"   Gap:             ${gap:,.0f} ({gap/avg_by_type['staff']*100:.1f}% higher)")
```
:::
:::

## Summary

You've learned web scraping fundamentals:

- **BeautifulSoup**: Parse HTML with `find()`, `find_all()`, and `select()`
- **CSS Selectors**: Powerful element selection with `.class`, `#id`, `parent child`
- **DOM Navigation**: Move through parent, child, and sibling elements
- **Table Extraction**: Parse HTML tables into DataFrames
- **Error Handling**: Deal with missing elements gracefully
- **Pagination**: Scrape multiple pages systematically
- **Caching**: Store pages locally to avoid re-fetching
- **Ethics**: Check robots.txt, use delays, identify yourself

Key principles:
- Always prefer APIs over scraping
- Be respectful: delays, caching, proper User-Agent
- Handle missing data gracefully
- Test on cached data during development

## Next Steps

Continue to [Lesson 17: Building Reusable Data Pipelines](17-reusable-pipelines.qmd) to learn how to structure your code for production use.
